title: Entanglement in quantum channel discrimination: sometimes less is more
authors: Kristin Sundal Lien, Marco Túlio Quintino
abstract: Entanglement is known to be a powerful resource that improves performance in various quantum information and computational tasks. A standard example of such a phenomenon is the possibility of perfectly discriminating all four Pauli operations in a single shot via the superdense coding protocol. While entanglement is often a powerful resource for quantum channel discrimination, this is not necessarily the case. In this work, we identify scenarios in which the maximally entangled state is a bad choice of input state and, more generally, show that excessive entanglement can reduce channel discriminability dramatically. To do so, we present an explicit pair of unitary channels which are perfectly discriminable without entanglement, but for which any strategy with maximally entangled input states is $ε$-close to a blind uniform guessing strategy. To develop a systematic approach, we introduce the concepts of Maximal Entanglement Worst Case (MEWC) and Maximal Entanglement Best Case (MEBC) pairs of channels, and present conditions for a pair of channels to be MEWC or MEBC. With these conditions, we show that the optimal input states for discriminating MEWC pairs of channels are necessarily separable, and provide non-trivial examples of measurement channels for which entanglement necessarily reduces the maximum probability of discrimination.
summary: This work explores the limits of entanglement in quantum channel discrimination, proving that entanglement is not always beneficial. By introducing the MEWC/MEBC framework, the authors demonstrate that for certain channel pairs, using maximally entangled states actively reduces the maximum achievable success probability compared to using separable input states.
main_problem: The paper investigates quantum channel discrimination, specifically identifying scenarios where the use of maximally entangled input states is suboptimal or actively detrimental to the discrimination success probability.
main_result: It establishes that for certain pairs of channels (MEWC pairs), the optimal input state must be separable, and excessive entanglement can significantly reduce the maximum achievable discrimination probability.
method: The analysis employs concepts like the Diamond Norm ($\|\cdot\|_\diamond$) and the ME-norm ($\|\cdot\|_{ME}$), introducing the Maximal Entanglement Worst Case (MEWC) and Best Case (MEBC) classifications to systematically characterize the role of entanglement.
model_or_system: The study models the task of distinguishing between two or more quantum channels ($\Phi_i$), using the Choi-Jamiołkowski isomorphism to translate the problem into analyzing the difference map $\Delta\Phi$. The analysis compares performance using separable vs. maximally entangled input states.
key_observables: Success probability of discrimination ($P^*_s$), the M-operator ($M(\Delta\Phi)$), and the relationship between the diamond norm and the ME-norm.
important_parameters: The entanglement entropy of the input state, the dimension of the Hilbert space ($d$), and the structure of the difference map $\Delta\Phi$.
main_assumptions: The analysis often focuses on the uniform prior probability case and compares the optimal strategy (using the diamond norm) against the maximally entangled strategy (using the ME-norm).
figures_summary: Figures illustrate the success probability for discriminating Pauli channels, showing an increase with entanglement, but also show cases where the probability decreases with increasing entanglement, favoring separable states.
paper_structure: The paper develops from general channel discrimination theory, introduces the MEWC/MEBC concepts, derives mathematical bounds relating different norms, and applies these concepts to specific examples like Pauli and measurement channels to prove when entanglement is detrimental.
why_interesting: It provides a rigorous theoretical framework for understanding when entanglement, typically viewed as a resource, fails to provide an advantage in quantum information tasks, which is crucial for designing practical quantum protocols.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Figure 1: Maximal success probability of discrimination between the four Pauli channels. Success probability of dis- crimination increases with increasing entanglement.
Figure 2: Maximal success probability of discrimination between Werner-Holevo channels. Success probability of discrimination increases with increasing entanglement.

Figure 3: Maximal success probability of discrimination between two measurement channels. Success probability of discrimination decreases with increasing entanglement. The top horizontal (green) dot-dashed line is the maximal suc- cess probability with separable states, the bottom horizontal (orange) dashed line is the maximal success probability with separable states, and the (blue) filled curve is the optimal success probability with a fixed value of entanglement entropy.

Figure 4: The figure shows three different types of strategies for measuring the channel Φ. The simplest strategy, shown in (a), uses no ancilla. The most general strategy, shown in (b), uses a bipartite input state ρAA′ that may or may not be entangled with the auxiliary system A′. In (c), a maximally entangled input state ϕ+ is used, and we effectively measure the Choi state J(Φ)

Figure 6: The eigenvalues of the unitary W = diag(−1, 1, 1, .., 1) on the complex unit circle. We see that the origin is within the convex hull of the eigenvalues, but the centroid approaches one as the dimension d increases.
